message.content:
Adversaries may exploit the "at" utility to schedule tasks for executing malicious code on Windows, Linux, and macOS systems. Despite being deprecated in favor of "schtasks" in Windows, "at" can still be used if the Task Scheduler service is active and the user has administrative privileges. On Linux and macOS, "at" can be invoked by the superuser or users listed in the "at.allow" file. If neither "at.allow" nor "at.deny" files exist, only the superuser can use "at."

The primary impact includes persistence, remote execution, lateral movement, and privilege escalation. Adversaries can schedule tasks to run at system startup or specific times, execute commands remotely, or run processes under privileged accounts. Notable groups like APT18, BRONZE BUTLER, and Threat Group-3390 have used "at" for these purposes.

Mitigation strategies include disabling or restricting the use of the "at" utility, monitoring task scheduling activities, and ensuring that only authorized users can create or modify scheduled tasks. Regular audits of task scheduler configurations and user permissions can also help detect and prevent abuse of the "at" utility.
